#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <wiringPi.h>
#include <wiringPiSPI.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <signal.h>

// Constants
#define SPI_CHANNEL 0          // SPI channel (CE0)
#define SPI_SPEED 1000000       // SPI speed (1 MHz)
#define ADC_CHANNEL 0           // MCP3008 channel connected to AWM5300V
#define VOLTAGE_THRESHOLD 2.0   // Voltage threshold for alarm (V)
#define ALARM_GPIO 18           // GPIO pin for alarm signal
#define LOG_FILE "airflow_log.txt"

// Global variables
FILE *log_file = NULL;
volatile sig_atomic_t program_running = 1;

// Function prototypes
float read_awm5300v();
void log_airflow(float voltage);
void trigger_alarm(int state);
void signal_handler(int signal);

// Function to read the analog value from the AWM5300V sensor via MCP3008 ADC
float read_awm5300v() {
    unsigned char buffer[3];
    int adc_value;
    float voltage;

    buffer[0] = 0x01;  // Start bit
    buffer[1] = ((ADC_CHANNEL & 0x07) << 4); // Single-ended mode, channel selection
    buffer[2] = 0x00;

    wiringPiSPIDataRW(SPI_CHANNEL, buffer, 3);

    adc_value = ((buffer[1] & 0x0F) << 8) + buffer[2];

    // Convert ADC value to voltage (assuming 3.3V reference)
    voltage = ((float)adc_value / 1023.0) * 3.3;

    return voltage;
}

// Function to log the airflow data to a file
void log_airflow(float voltage) {
    time_t current_time;
    struct tm *time_info;
    char timestamp[20];

    // Get current time
    time(&current_time);
    time_info = localtime(&current_time);

    // Format timestamp
    strftime(timestamp, sizeof(timestamp), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", time_info);

    // Open log file in append mode
    log_file = fopen(LOG_FILE, "a");
    if (log_file == NULL) {
        perror("Error opening log file");
        return;
    }

    // Write data to log file
    fprintf(log_file, "%s,%.4f\n", timestamp, voltage);

    // Close log file
    fclose(log_file);
    log_file = NULL; // Reset log_file pointer

    printf("%s,%.4f\n", timestamp, voltage); // Also print to console
}

// Function to trigger the alarm based on the airflow threshold
void trigger_alarm(int state) {
    digitalWrite(ALARM_GPIO, state);
    printf("Alarm %s\n", state ? "ON" : "OFF");
}

// Signal handler function to handle Ctrl+C and other termination signals
void signal_handler(int signal) {
    printf("Caught signal %d, exiting...\n", signal);
    program_running = 0;
    trigger_alarm(0); // Turn off alarm before exiting

    if (log_file != NULL) {
        fclose(log_file);
    }
    exit(0);
}

int main() {
    // Initialize wiringPi
    if (wiringPiSetupGpio() == -1) { // Use GPIO numbering
        fprintf(stderr, "wiringPiSetupGpio failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Initialize SPI
    if (wiringPiSPISetup(SPI_CHANNEL, SPI_SPEED) == -1) {
        fprintf(stderr, "wiringPiSPISetup failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Set alarm GPIO as output
    pinMode(ALARM_GPIO, OUTPUT);
    trigger_alarm(0); // Ensure alarm is initially off

    // Set up signal handling for Ctrl+C and other termination signals
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);
    signal(SIGTERM, signal_handler);

    // Main loop
    while (program_running) {
        float voltage = read_awm5300v();

        if (voltage < VOLTAGE_THRESHOLD) {
            trigger_alarm(1); // Turn on alarm
        } else {
            trigger_alarm(0); // Turn off alarm
        }

        log_airflow(voltage);
        delay(1000); // Wait 1 second
    }

    return 0;
}<|im_end|>

```c
            digitalWrite(ALARM_PIN, HIGH);  // Trigger the alarm
            alarmStatus = 1;
        } else if (voltage >= AIRFLOW_THRESHOLD && alarmStatus) {
            digitalWrite(ALARM_PIN, LOW);   // Turn off the alarm
            alarmStatus = 0;
        }

        // Sleep for 1 second before the next reading
        sleep(1);
    }

    return 0;
}
```

### Explanation:
1. **Include Headers**: The necessary headers are included for file operations, time handling, and GPIO operations.
2. **Define Constants**: Constants for the sensor pin, threshold voltage, and alarm pin are defined.
3. **Log Function**: `log_data` function writes the timestamp and voltage to the log file.
4. **Main Function**:
   - Initializes `wiringPi`.
   - Sets up the GPIO pins for input and output.
   - Enters an infinite loop where it reads the sensor value, converts it to voltage, logs the data, and checks if the voltage is below the threshold.
   - Triggers the alarm if the voltage is below the threshold and turns it off when the voltage exceeds the threshold.
   - Sleeps for 1 second between readings.

### Compilation and Running:
Compile the program using `gcc`:

```sh
gcc -o airflow_sensor airflow_sensor.c -lwiringPi
```

Run the program:

```sh
sudo ./airflow_sensor
```

Make sure to give the program sufficient permissions to access GPIO pins and files.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 3 Model B+ to interface with a Honeywell AWM5300V airflow sensor (analog voltage output) using the wiringPi library, logging airflow readings to "airflow_log.txt" with timestamp and voltage, triggering an alarm via GPIO if airflow drops below a threshold.

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <wiringPi.h>
#include <wiringPiSPI.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <signal.h>
#include <unistd.h>

// Constants
#define SPI_CHANNEL 0
#define SPI_SPEED 1000000 // 1 MHz
#define ADC_CHANNEL 0     // MCP3008 channel connected to AWM5300V
#define ALARM_GPIO 18      // GPIO pin for alarm signal
#define AIRFLOW_THRESHOLD 20.0 // Airflow threshold in liters per minute
#define VREF 3.3           // Reference voltage of MCP3008

// Function prototypes
float read_airflow();
void log_data(float voltage, float airflow);
void trigger_alarm();
void signal_handler(int signal);

int main() {
    // Initialize wiringPi
    if (wiringPiSetupGpio() == -1) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error initializing wiringPi.\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Initialize SPI
    if (wiringPiSPISetup(SPI_CHANNEL, SPI_SPEED) == -1) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error initializing SPI.\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Set alarm GPIO as output
    pinMode(ALARM_GPIO, OUTPUT);

    // Register signal handler for Ctrl+C
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);

    printf("Airflow monitoring started. Press Ctrl+C to exit.\n");

    while (1) {
        float voltage = read_airflow();
        float airflow = voltage_to_airflow(voltage);
        log_data(voltage, airflow);

        if (airflow < AIRFLOW_THRESHOLD) {
            trigger_alarm();
        }

        delay(1000); // Read every 1 second
    }

    return 0;
}

// Function to read airflow from AWM5300V sensor
float read_airflow() {
    unsigned char buffer[3];
    int adc_value;

    // Read from MCP3008
    buffer[0] = 0x01;  // Start bit
    buffer[1] = (0x08 | ADC_CHANNEL) << 4; // Single-ended mode, channel selection
    buffer[2] = 0x00;

    wiringPiSPIDataRW(SPI_CHANNEL, buffer, 3);

    adc_value = ((buffer[1] & 0x03) << 8) + buffer[2];

    // Convert ADC value to voltage
    float voltage = ((float)adc_value / 1023.0) * VREF;

    return voltage;
}

// Function to convert voltage to airflow (LPM)
float voltage_to_airflow(float voltage) {
    // Linear relationship: 0.6V = 0 LPM, 3.3V = 50 LPM
    return (voltage - 0.6) * (50.0 / (3.3 - 0.6));
}

// Function to log data to file
void log_data(float voltage, float airflow) {
    FILE *fp;
    time_t t = time(NULL);
    struct tm tm = *localtime(&t);

    fp = fopen("airflow_log.txt", "a");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error opening airflow_log.txt for writing.\n");
        return;
    }

    fprintf(fp, "%d-%02d-%02d %02d:%02d:%02d, Voltage: %.3f V, Airflow: %.2f LPM\n",
            tm.tm_year + 1900, tm.tm_mon + 1, tm.tm_mday, tm.tm_hour, tm.tm_min, tm.tm_sec,
            voltage, airflow);

    fclose(fp);
}

// Function to trigger alarm
void trigger_alarm() {
    digitalWrite(ALARM_GPIO, HIGH); // Turn on alarm
    delay(5000);                    // Keep on for 5 seconds
    digitalWrite(ALARM_GPIO, LOW);  // Turn off alarm
}

// Signal handler for Ctrl+C
void signal_handler(int signal) {
    printf("\nExiting gracefully...\n");
    digitalWrite(ALARM_GPIO, LOW); // Ensure alarm is off
    exit(0);
}<|im_end|>

To create a C program for a Raspberry Pi 5 that uses the `pigpio` library to control two KY-012 active buzzer modules, we need to follow these steps:

1. Initialize the `pigpio` library.
2. Set up the GPIO pins for the buzzers.
3. Read distance values from standard input.
4. Based on the distance, trigger different alarm patterns on the buzzers.
5. Handle errors for `pigpio` initialization and invalid inputs.

Here is the complete code for this task:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

#define BUZZER_PIN_1 17
#define BUZZER_PIN_2 18

// Function prototypes
int initialize_pigpio(void);
void cleanup_pigpio(int status);
void play_alarm(double distance);

int main() {
    int status;

    // Initialize pigpio
    if ((status = initialize_pigpio()) != PI_OK) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to initialize pigpio: %s\n", pigpio_error(status));
        return 1;
    }

    // Set up buzzer pins as PWM output
    gpioSetMode(BUZZER_PIN_1, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetPWMfrequency(BUZZER_PIN_1, 440); // Set frequency to 440 Hz
    gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 0); // Turn off buzzer

    gpioSetMode(BUZZER_PIN_2, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetPWMfrequency(BUZZER_PIN_2, 440); // Set frequency to 440 Hz
    gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 0); // Turn off buzzer

    while (1) {
        double distance;
        char line[100];

        // Read distance value from stdin
        printf("Enter distance in cm (0-100): ");
        if (!fgets(line, sizeof(line), stdin)) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Error reading input.\n");
            break;
        }

        if (sscanf(line, "%lf", &distance) != 1 || distance < 0 || distance > 100) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Invalid input! Please enter a number between 0 and 100.\n");
            continue;
        }

        // Play appropriate alarm based on distance
        play_alarm(distance);
    }

    // Cleanup
    cleanup_pigpio(0);

    return 0;
}

int initialize_pigpio(void) {
    return gpioInitialise();
}

void cleanup_pigpio(int status) {
    gpioTerminate();
    exit(status);
}

void play_alarm(double distance) {
    if (distance < 10.0) {
        // Critical low threshold
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 1000); // High volume
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 1000); // High volume
        usleep(500000); // 0.5 seconds
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 0);
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 0);
        usleep(500000); // 0.5 seconds
    } else if (distance < 20.0) {
        // Low threshold
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 500); // Medium volume
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 500); // Medium volume
        usleep(250000); // 0.25 seconds
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 0);
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 0);
        usleep(250000); // 0.25 seconds
    } else {
        // Normal operation
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 0);
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 0);
    }
}
```

### Explanation:
1. **Initialization and Cleanup**: The `initialize_pigpio` function initializes the `pigpio` library, and `cleanup_pigpio` terminates it.
2. **GPIO Setup**: The buzzer pins are set up as PWM outputs.
3. **Input Handling**: The program reads a distance value from the user and checks if it's within the valid range (0-100 cm).
4. **Alarm Patterns**: Different alarm patterns are played based on the distance:
   - Below 10 cm: High-volume continuous beeping.
   - Between 10 cm and 20 cm: Medium-volume intermittent beeping.
   - Above 20 cm: No sound (normal operation).

### Notes:
- Ensure you have the `pigpio` library installed on your Raspberry Pi 5.
- Run the program with root privileges or configure `pigpio` to allow non-root users.
- Adjust the buzzer frequencies and durations as needed for your specific application.<|im_end|>

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <signal.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

// Define GPIO pins for the buzzers
#define BUZZER1_PIN 17
#define BUZZER2_PIN 18

// Function prototypes
int read_distance();
void generate_frequency(int frequency);
void generate_alarm1();
void generate_alarm2();
void cleanup(int signal);

// Global variables
volatile sig_atomic_t terminate = 0;

// Signal handler function
void cleanup(int signal) {
    printf("Cleaning up and exiting...\n");
    gpioTerminate();
    exit(0);
}

// Function to read distance from stdin
int read_distance() {
    int distance;
    char input[3];

    // Read input from stdin
    if (fgets(input, sizeof(input), stdin) == NULL) {
        return -1; // Indicate error (e.g., EOF)
    }

    // Parse the input as an integer
    if (sscanf(input, "%d", &distance) != 1) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Invalid input: %s\n", input);
        return -1; // Indicate parsing error
    }

    return distance;
}

// Function to generate a square wave at a specified frequency
void generate_frequency(int frequency) {
    gpioSetMode(BUZZER1_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(BUZZER2_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);

    double period = 1.0 / frequency;
    double half_period = period / 2.0;
    long int delay_us = (long int)(half_period * 1000000.0);

    while (!terminate) {
        gpioWrite(BUZZER1_PIN, 1);
        gpioDelay(delay_us);
        gpioWrite(BUZZER1_PIN, 0);
        gpioDelay(delay_us);

        gpioWrite(BUZZER2_PIN, 1);
        gpioDelay(delay_us);
        gpioWrite(BUZZER2_PIN, 0);
        gpioDelay(delay_us);
    }
}

// Function to generate alarm 1 (higher frequency)
void generate_alarm1() {
    generate_frequency(2000); // 2 kHz
}

// Function to generate alarm 2 (lower frequency)
void generate_alarm2() {
    generate_frequency(400);  // 400 Hz
}

int main() {
    // Initialize pigpio library
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Set up signal handlers for graceful shutdown
    signal(SIGINT, cleanup);
    signal(SIGTERM, cleanup);

    printf("Proximity alert system started. Enter distance (0-100cm). Press Ctrl+C to exit.\n");

    while (!terminate) {
        int distance = read_distance();

        if (distance == -1) {
            // Handle input errors (EOF or parsing error)
            gpioTerminate();
            return 1;
        }

        if (distance >= 0 && distance <= 100) {
            if (distance < 20) {
                generate_alarm2();
            } else if (distance < 10) {
                generate_alarm1();
            } else {
                // Stop the alarms for distances greater than 10cm
                gpioWrite(BUZZER1_PIN, 0);
                gpioWrite(BUZZER2_PIN, 0);
            }
        } else {
            fprintf(stderr, "Distance out of range (0-100cm): %d\n", distance);
        }

        gpioDelay(500000); // Delay for 0.5 seconds
    }

    // Cleanup and exit
    gpioTerminate();
    return 0;
}<|im_end|>

```c
#include <stdio.h>

#define BUZZER_PIN_2 18

// Function prototypes
int initialize_pigpio(void);
void cleanup_pigpio(void);

int main() {
    // Initialize pigpio
    if (!initialize_pigpio()) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to initialize pigpio\n");
        return EXIT_FAILURE;

    int distance;
    while (1) {

            gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 500);
        } else if (distance <= 20) {
```

#define DISTANCE_TRIGGER_1 20 // cm
#define DISTANCE_TRIGGER_2 10 // cm

// Function prototypes
int parse_distance(char *input);
void set_frequency(int gpio_pin, int frequency);
void alarm_pattern_1();
void alarm_pattern_2();
void stop_alarm();
void signal_handler(int signal);

// Global variable to indicate program termination
volatile sig_atomic_t terminate = 0;

// Signal handler function
void signal_handler(int signal)
{
    if (signal == SIGINT) {
        printf("Caught SIGINT, terminating program...\n");
        terminate = 1;
    }
}

// Function to parse the input string representing the distance
int parse_distance(char *input)
{
    char *endptr;
    long result = strtol(input, &endptr, 10);

    // Check for errors during conversion
    if (result == 0 && *endptr == '\0') {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error: Invalid input format.\n");
        return -1;
    }

    // Validate the distance range
    if (result < 0 || result > 100) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error: Distance out of range (0-100cm).\n");
        return -1;
    }

    return (int)result;
}

// Function to set the frequency of the buzzer connected to the specified GPIO pin
void set_frequency(int gpio_pin, int frequency)
{
    gpioSetPWMfrequency(gpio_pin, frequency);
    gpioPWM(gpio_pin, 128); // 50% duty cycle
}

// Function to generate a simple alarm pattern (short beep)
void alarm_pattern_1()
{
    set_frequency(BUZZER_1, 1000);
    gpioDelay(500000); // 0.5 seconds
    set_frequency(BUZZER_1, 0);
    gpioDelay(500000); // 0.5 seconds
}

// Function to generate a more complex alarm pattern (long beep followed by short beep)
void alarm_pattern_2()
{
    set_frequency(BUZZER_2, 2000);
    gpioDelay(1000000); // 1 second
    set_frequency(BUZZER_2, 0);
    gpioDelay(500000);  // 0.5 seconds
    set_frequency(BUZZER_2, 500);
    gpioDelay(500000);  // 0.5 seconds
    set_frequency(BUZZER_2, 0);
    gpioDelay(500000);  // 0.5 seconds
}

// Function to stop the alarm (turn off the buzzer)
void stop_alarm()
{
    gpioPWM(BUZZER_1, 0);
    gpioPWM(BUZZER_2, 0);
}

int main()
{
    // Initialize the pigpio library
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error: pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Configure GPIO pins as output
    gpioSetMode(BUZZER_1, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(BUZZER_2, PI_OUTPUT);

    // Set up signal handler for Ctrl+C
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);

    printf("Proximity Alert System running...\n");

    while (!terminate) {
        char input[64];
        printf("Enter distance (0-100cm): ");
        if (fgets(input, sizeof(input), stdin) == NULL) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Error reading input\n");
            continue; // Skip to the next iteration
        }

        int distance = parse_distance(input);
        if (distance == -1) {
            continue; // Skip to the next iteration if parsing fails
        }

        // Determine which alarm pattern to trigger based on the distance
        if (distance < DISTANCE_TRIGGER_1) {
            alarm_pattern_1();
        } else if (distance < DISTANCE_TRIGGER_2) {
            alarm_pattern_2();
        } else {
            stop_alarm();
        }

        gpioDelay(500000); // Debounce delay (0.5 seconds)
    }

    // Clean up and terminate the pigpio library before exiting
    stop_alarm();
    gpioTerminate();
    printf("Program terminated.\n");

    return 0;
}<|im_end|>

To create a rudimentary proximity alert system using a Raspberry Pi 5 and the `pigpio` library, we'll follow these steps:

1. Initialize the `pigpio` library.
2. Set up the GPIO pins for the KY-012 active buzzer modules.
3. Read distance values from standard input (stdin) in the range of 0 to 100 cm.
4. Adjust the frequency of the buzzer based on the simulated distance.
5. Trigger specific alarm patterns if the distance is less than 20 cm or 10 cm.
6. Implement error handling for `pigpio` initialization and invalid input.

Here's the C code to achieve this:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

#define BEEPER_PIN_1 17
#define BEEPER_PIN_2 18

void set_buzzer_frequency(int pin, int freq) {
    gpioSetPWMFreq(pin, 50); // Set PWM frequency to 50 Hz
    gpioPWM(pin, freq);
}

int main() {
    int dist;
    int ret;

    // Initialize pigpio library
    if ((ret = gpioInitialise()) < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialization failed: %s\n", pigpioGetError(ret));
        return -1;
    }

    // Set up GPIO pins as outputs
    gpioSetMode(BEEPER_PIN_1, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(BEEPER_PIN_2, PI_OUTPUT);

    while (1) {
        printf("Enter distance (0-100): ");
        if (scanf("%d", &dist) != 1 || dist < 0 || dist > 100) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Invalid input. Please enter a value between 0 and 100.\n");
            continue;
        }

        if (dist >= 20) {
            set_buzzer_frequency(BEEPER_PIN_1, 2000); // Frequency for distances 20-100 cm
            set_buzzer_frequency(BEEPER_PIN_2, 2000);
        } else if (dist >= 10) {
            set_buzzer_frequency(BEEPER_PIN_1, 4000); // Frequency for distances 10-19 cm
            set_buzzer_frequency(BEEPER_PIN_2, 4000);
        } else {
            set_buzzer_frequency(BEEPER_PIN_1, 8000); // Frequency for distances 0-9 cm
            set_buzzer_frequency(BEEPER_PIN_2, 8000);
        }
    }

    // Clean up
    gpioTerminate();

    return 0;
}
```

### Explanation:
1. **Initialization**: The `pigpio` library is initialized using `gpioInitialise()`. If initialization fails, an error message is printed, and the program exits.
2. **GPIO Setup**: The GPIO pins 17 and 18 are configured as outputs.
3. **Distance Input Loop**: The program enters a loop where it continuously reads distance values from the user. It validates the input to ensure it's within the specified range (0-100).
4. **Frequency Adjustment**: Based on the distance, the frequency of the beeps is adjusted. Distances above 20 cm use a lower frequency, 10-20 cm use a medium frequency, and distances below 10 cm use a higher frequency.
5. **Alarm Patterns**: When the distance falls below 20 cm or 10 cm, the frequency of both buzzers increases to indicate the proximity.
6. **Cleanup**: The `pigpio` library is terminated using `gpioTerminate()` to clean up resources.

### Notes:
- Ensure the `pigpio` library is installed on your Raspberry Pi before compiling this program.
- Compile the program with a command like `gcc -o proximity_alert proximity_alert.c -lpigpio`.
- Run the compiled program with `./proximity_alert`.

This program provides a basic framework for a proximity alert system using the KY-012 buzzer modules and the `pigpio` library on a Raspberry Pi 5.<|im_end|>

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <signal.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <math.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

// Define GPIO pins for the active buzzer modules
#define BUZZER1_PIN 17
#define BUZZER2_PIN 18

// Define frequency ranges for different distance ranges
#define MIN_FREQUENCY 200
#define MAX_FREQUENCY 800

// Global flag to signal program termination
volatile sig_atomic_t terminate = 0;

// Signal handler function
void signal_handler(int sig) {
    fprintf(stderr, "Signal received (%d). Terminating program...\n", sig);
    terminate = 1;
}

// Function to generate a sine wave with a given frequency and duty cycle
void generate_sine_wave(int gpio_pin, int frequency, int period_us, int duty_cycle_percent) {
    // Calculate pulse width in microseconds
    int pulse_width = (int)(((double)duty_cycle_percent / 100.0) * period_us);

    // Calculate on/off times
    int on_time = pulse_width;
    int off_time = period_us - pulse_width;

    // Generate the sine wave
    for (int i = 0; i < period_us; i++) {
        // Calculate the sine value (0 to 1)
        double t = (double)i / period_us;
        double sine_value = sin(2 * M_PI * frequency * t);

        // Map the sine value to the duty cycle range
        int pwm_value = (int)((sine_value + 1) / 2 * 1000); // Scale to 0-1000

        // Set PWM value
        gpioPWM(gpio_pin, pwm_value);

        // Wait for a short period
        usleep(1000); // 1ms delay
    }
}

// Function to set the buzzer frequency
void set_buzzer_frequency(int gpio_pin, int frequency) {
    // Ensure frequency is within valid range
    if (frequency < MIN_FREQUENCY || frequency > MAX_FREQUENCY) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error: Frequency %d out of range (%d-%d).\n", frequency, MIN_FREQUENCY, MAX_FREQUENCY);
        return;
    }

    // Calculate period in microseconds
    int period_us = 1000000 / frequency;

    // Generate sine wave
    generate_sine_wave(gpio_pin, frequency, period_us, 50); // 50% duty cycle
}

// Function to simulate distance reading from stdin
int get_distance() {
    char buffer[3];
    ssize_t bytes_read = read(STDIN_FILENO, buffer, sizeof(buffer) - 1);

    if (bytes_read == -1) {
        perror("Error reading from stdin");
        return -1; // Indicate an error
    } else if (bytes_read == 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "End of input reached.\n");
        return -1; // Indicate end of input
    }

    buffer[bytes_read] = '\0'; // Null-terminate the string
    return atoi(buffer);
}

int main() {
    // Register signal handlers for graceful termination
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);
    signal(SIGTERM, signal_handler);

    // Initialize pigpio library
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Set GPIO pins as outputs
    gpioSetMode(BUZZER1_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(BUZZER2_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);

    // Main loop
    while (!terminate) {
        // Get distance reading
        int distance = get_distance();
        if (distance == -1) {
            break; // Exit loop on error or end of input
        }

        // Determine buzzer behavior based on distance
        if (distance < 10) {
            // Critical alert (very low distance)
            printf("Critical alert! Distance: %d cm\n", distance);
            set_buzzer_frequency(BUZZER1_PIN, 1000); // High frequency
            set_buzzer_frequency(BUZZER2_PIN, 500);  // Lower frequency
            usleep(2000000); // 2 seconds
            set_buzzer_frequency(BUZZER1_PIN, 0);   // Silence buzzer 1
            set_buzzer_frequency(BUZZER2_PIN, 0);   // Silence buzzer 2
        } else if (distance < 20) {
            // Warning alert (low distance)
            printf("Warning! Distance: %d cm\n", distance);
            set_buzzer_frequency(BUZZER1_PIN, 1000); // High frequency
            set_buzzer_frequency(BUZZER2_PIN, 0);   // Silent buzzer 2
            usleep(1000000); // 1 second
            set_buzzer_frequency(BUZZER1_PIN, 0);   // Silence buzzer 1
            set_buzzer_frequency(BUZZER2_PIN, 0);   // Silence buzzer 2
        } else {
            // Normal operation (moderate distance)
            printf("Distance: %d cm\n", distance);
            set_buzzer_frequency(BUZZER1_PIN, 400);  // Medium frequency
            set_buzzer_frequency(BUZZER2_PIN, 600);  // Medium frequency
            usleep(500000);  // 0.5 seconds
            set_buzzer_frequency(BUZZER1_PIN, 0);   // Silence buzzer 1
            set_buzzer_frequency(BUZZER2_PIN, 0);   // Silence buzzer 2
        }
    }

    // Terminate pigpio library
    gpioTerminate();

    return 0;
}<|im_end|>

The program will trigger specific alarm patterns when the distance falls below 20cm or 10cm.

First, ensure you have the `pigpio` library installed. You can install it using the following command:

```bash
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install pigpio
```

Next, we will write the C program. Here's a step-by-step guide to creating this program:

1. Include necessary headers.
2. Initialize the `pigpio` library.
3. Read distance values from stdin.
4. Control the buzzer frequencies based on the distance.
5. Handle errors gracefully.

Here is the complete C code:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <pigpio.h>
#include <string.h>

#define BEEPER_PIN_1 17
#define BEEPER_PIN_2 18

void handle_error(const char *message) {
    fprintf(stderr, "Error: %s\n", message);
    exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
}

int main() {
    // Initialize pigpio library
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        handle_error("Failed to initialize pigpio");
    }

    // Set up GPIO pins for PWM output
    gpioSetMode(BEEPER_PIN_1, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetPWMfrequency(BEEPER_PIN_1, 440); // Initial frequency for beeper 1
    gpioSetPWMrange(BEEPER_PIN_1, 2000);

    gpioSetMode(BEEPER_PIN_2, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetPWMfrequency(BEEPER_PIN_2, 660); // Initial frequency for beeper 2
    gpioSetPWMrange(BEEPER_PIN_2, 2000);

    while (1) {
        int distance;
        printf("Enter distance in cm (0-100): ");
        if (scanf("%d", &distance) != 1) {
            handle_error("Invalid input");
        }

        if (distance < 0 || distance > 100) {
            handle_error("Distance must be between 0 and 100");
        }

        // Calculate frequency based on distance
        float frequency = 440 + (distance / 2.5); // Adjust as needed

        if (frequency > 880) {
            frequency = 880; // Cap frequency at 880Hz
        } else if (frequency < 220) {
            frequency = 220; // Start frequency at 220Hz
        }

        // Set PWM frequency for both beepers
        gpioPWM(BEEPER_PIN_1, (int)frequency);
        gpioPWM(BEEPER_PIN_2, (int)frequency);

        // Trigger distinct alarm patterns
        if (distance < 20) {
            gpioPWM(BEEPER_PIN_1, 220);
            gpioPWM(BEEPER_PIN_2, 220);
            sleep(1);
            gpioPWM(BEEPER_PIN_1, 440);
            gpioPWM(BEEPER_PIN_2, 440);
            sleep(1);
        } else if (distance < 10) {
            gpioPWM(BEEPER_PIN_1, 880);
            gpioPWM(BEEPER_PIN_2, 880);
            sleep(1);
            gpioPWM(BEEPER_PIN_1, 660);
            gpioPWM(BEEPER_PIN_2, 660);
            sleep(1);
        }
    }

    // Clean up GPIO settings
    gpioTerminate();

    return 0;
}
```

### Explanation:
1. **Headers and Definitions**: Includes necessary headers and defines constants for the GPIO pins.
2. **Error Handling Function**: A function to print an error message and exit the program.

void signal_handler(int signal) {
    if (signal == SIGINT) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Caught SIGINT, exiting gracefully.\n");
        terminate = 1;
    }
}

// Reads distance from stdin and returns it as an integer.
int read_distance() {
    char buffer[10];
    int distance = -1; // Default value in case of error

    if (fgets(buffer, sizeof(buffer), stdin) != NULL) {
        if (sscanf(buffer, "%d", &distance) == 1 && distance >= 0 && distance <= 100) {
            return distance;
        } else {
            fprintf(stderr, "Invalid input: %s\n", buffer);
            return -1;
        }
    } else {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error reading from stdin: %s\n", strerror(errno));
        return -1;
    }
}

// Sets the frequency of the buzzer.
void set_buzzer_frequency(Buzzer *buzzer, int frequency) {
    if (frequency > 0 && frequency <= 10000) {
        gpioSetPWMfrequency(buzzer->gpio_pin, frequency);
        buzzer->frequency = frequency;
    } else {
        fprintf(stderr, "Invalid frequency: %d\n", frequency);
    }
}

// Triggers a specific alarm pattern for a given buzzer.
void trigger_alarm(Buzzer *buzzer, int frequency) {
    printf("Triggering alarm on buzzer at pin %d with frequency %d Hz\n", buzzer->gpio_pin, frequency);
    set_buzzer_frequency(buzzer, frequency);
    gpioPWM(buzzer->gpio_pin, 128); // Start PWM signal
    sleep(2);
    gpioPWM(buzzer->gpio_pin, 0);  // Stop PWM signal
    set_buzzer_frequency(buzzer, 0); // Reset frequency to zero
}

// Stops the alarm by turning off the buzzer.
void stop_alarm(Buzzer *buzzer) {
    printf("Stopping alarm on buzzer at pin %d\n", buzzer->gpio_pin);
    set_buzzer_frequency(buzzer, 0); // Ensure frequency is reset to zero
    gpioPWM(buzzer->gpio_pin, 0);  // Turn off PWM signal
}

int main() {
    // Initialize pigpio
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed: %s\n", strerror(errno));
        return 1;
    }

    // Set signal handler for Ctrl+C
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);

    // Configure GPIO pins as outputs
    gpioSetMode(buzzer1.gpio_pin, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(buzzer2.gpio_pin, PI_OUTPUT);

    // Initialize buzzer frequencies to 0
    set_buzzer_frequency(&buzzer1, 0);
    set_buzzer_frequency(&buzzer2, 0);

    printf("Proximity alert system started. Press Ctrl+C to exit.\n");

    while (!terminate) {
        int distance = read_distance();

        if (distance >= 0) {
            if (distance <= 10) {
                trigger_alarm(&buzzer1, 300);
                trigger_alarm(&buzzer2, 600);
            } else if (distance <= 20) {
                trigger_alarm(&buzzer1, 400);
                trigger_alarm(&buzzer2, 500);
            } else {
                stop_alarm(&buzzer1);
                stop_alarm(&buzzer2);
            }
        } else {
            // Handle invalid input or stdin errors
            stop_alarm(&buzzer1);
            stop_alarm(&buzzer2);
        }

        gpioDelay(50000); // Delay for 50ms (avoid busy-waiting)
    }

    // Cleanup before exiting
    printf("Cleaning up...\n");
    stop_alarm(&buzzer1);
    stop_alarm(&buzzer2);
    gpioTerminate();

    return 0;
}<|im_end|>

To create a rudimentary proximity alert system using the KY-012 active buzzer modules on a Raspberry Pi 5, we will use the `pigpio` library for GPIO manipulation. Below is a complete C program that achieves this functionality:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

// Constants for GPIO pins and buzzer frequencies
#define BEEPER_PIN1 17
#define BEEPER_PIN2 18
#define LOW_FREQ 400  // Lower frequency for closer distances
#define HIGH_FREQ 800 // Higher frequency for farther distances

// Function prototypes
int get_distance_from_stdin();
void init_buzzers();
void set_buzzer_frequency(int pin, int freq);
void cleanup();

int main() {
    // Initialize pigpio
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Unable to initialize pigpio\n");
        return EXIT_FAILURE;
    }

    // Initialize buzzers
    init_buzzers();

    int distance;

    while (1) {
        distance = get_distance_from_stdin();

        if (distance >= 20) {
            set_buzzer_frequency(BEEPER_PIN1, HIGH_FREQ);
            set_buzzer_frequency(BEEPER_PIN2, HIGH_FREQ);
        } else if (distance >= 10) {
            set_buzzer_frequency(BEEPER_PIN1, LOW_FREQ);
            set_buzzer_frequency(BEEPER_PIN2, LOW_FREQ);
        } else {
            set_buzzer_frequency(BEEPER_PIN1, 0); // Turn off
            set_buzzer_frequency(BEEPER_PIN2, 0); // Turn off
        }
    }

    cleanup();
    return EXIT_SUCCESS;
}

int get_distance_from_stdin() {
    char line[100];
    printf("Enter distance (0-100cm): ");
    if (!fgets(line, sizeof(line), stdin)) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error reading from stdin\n");
        exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }

    char *endptr;
    long dist = strtof(line, &endptr);

    if (*endptr != '\n' || dist < 0 || dist > 100) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Invalid input, please enter a number between 0 and 100.\n");
        exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }

    return (int)dist;
}

void init_buzzers() {
    gpioSetMode(BEEPER_PIN1, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(BEEPER_PIN2, PI_OUTPUT);
}

void set_buzzer_frequency(int pin, int freq) {
    gpioFrequency(pin, freq);
}

void cleanup() {
    gpioTerminate();
}
```

### Explanation:

1. **Constants**: Define the GPIO pins and frequencies for the buzzers.
2. **Function Prototypes**: Declare helper functions for initializing the buzzer, setting the frequency, getting distance from stdin, and cleaning up resources.

   - Initializes `pigpio`.

   - Prompts the user to enter a distance.
   - Reads the input from stdin.
   - Validates the input to ensure it's within the range of 0-100cm.
5. **init_buzzers**:
   - Sets the GPIO mode for the buzzer pins as output.
6. **set_buzzer_frequency**:
   - Sets the frequency of the specified buzzer pin.
7. **cleanup**:
   - Cleans up resources by terminating the `pigpio` library.

### Compilation:
To compile this program, you need to have the `pigpio` development libraries installed. You can install them using the following command:

```sh
sudo apt-get install pigpio-dev
```

Then, compile the program using:

```sh
gcc -o proximity_alert proximity_alert.c -lpigpio
```

Run the compiled program:

```sh

```

This program will continuously prompt you to enter a distance and adjust the buzzer frequencies accordingly.<|im_end|>

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <signal.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/socket.h>
#include <netinet/in.h>
#include <arpa/inet.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

#define BUZZER_GPIO_1 17
#define BUZZER_GPIO_2 18
#define PORT 12345

volatile sig_atomic_t running = 1;

void signal_handler(int signum) {
    if (signum == SIGINT) {
        printf("Received SIGINT. Shutting down...\n");
        running = 0;
    }
}

// Function to generate frequency-modulated sound
void playSound(int gpio, int frequency, int duration_ms) {
    for (int i = 0; i < duration_ms * 1000 / 50; i++) { // Convert ms to us
        gpioPWM(gpio, 255);
        usleep(50000 / frequency); // Calculate delay based on frequency
    }
    gpioPWM(gpio, 0); // Stop PWM
}

// Function to handle socket connection and data processing
void handleSocket(int client_socket) {
    char buffer[1024] = {0};
    ssize_t bytes_received = recv(client_socket, buffer, sizeof(buffer) - 1, 0);

    if (bytes_received > 0) {
        buffer[bytes_received] = '\0';
        int distance = atoi(buffer);

        if (distance < 0 || distance > 100) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Invalid distance reading: %d\n", distance);
            send(client_socket, "ERROR: Invalid distance\n", strlen("ERROR: Invalid distance\n"), 0);
            return;
        }

        if (distance < 20) {
            printf("Proximity Alert! Distance: %d cm\n", distance);
            playSound(BUZZER_GPIO_1, 200 + (distance * 2), 1000); // Low frequency
        } else if (distance < 10) {
            printf("Emergency Alert! Distance: %d cm\n", distance);
            playSound(BUZZER_GPIO_2, 800 + (distance * 10), 2000); // High frequency
        } else {
            // No alert
        }
        send(client_socket, "OK: Distance processed\n", strlen("OK: Distance processed\n"), 0);
    } else if (bytes_received == 0) {
        printf("Client disconnected.\n");
    } else {
        perror("recv failed");
        send(client_socket, "ERROR: Network error\n", strlen("ERROR: Network error\n"), 0);
    }

    close(client_socket);
}

int main() {
    // Install signal handler for Ctrl+C
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);

    // Initialize pigpio
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Set GPIO modes
    gpioSetMode(BUZZER_GPIO_1, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(BUZZER_GPIO_2, PI_OUTPUT);

    // Create socket
    int server_fd, new_socket;
    struct sockaddr_in address;
    int addrlen = sizeof(address);

    if ((server_fd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0)) == 0) {
        perror("socket failed");
        gpioTerminate();
        return 1;
    }

    address.sin_family = AF_INET;
    address.sin_addr.s_addr = INADDR_ANY;
    address.sin_port = htons(PORT);

    // Bind socket to address
    if (bind(server_fd, (struct sockaddr *)&address, sizeof(address)) < 0) {
        perror("bind failed");
        close(server_fd);
        gpioTerminate();
        return 1;
    }

    // Listen for connections
    if (listen(server_fd, 3) < 0) {
        perror("listen failed");
        close(server_fd);
        gpioTerminate();
        return 1;
    }

    printf("Server listening on port %d\n", PORT);

    while (running) {
        // Accept incoming connection
        if ((new_socket = accept(server_fd, (struct sockaddr *)&address, (socklen_t*)&addrlen)) < 0) {
            perror("accept failed");
            continue; // Continue listening
        }

        // Handle the connection in a separate thread or process
        handleSocket(new_socket);
    }

    // Cleanup
    close(server_fd);
    gpioTerminate();

    return 0;
}<|im_end|>

2.  **Button Debouncing:** Implement debouncing for the push button using `gpioSetAlertFuncEx()` on GPIO 4. This function should call a separate function when the button state changes, which handles the logic for triggering the door locks.

3.  **Motion Sensor Integration:** Monitor GPIO 22 (PIR sensor) using `gpioSetAlertFuncEx()`. When motion is detected (GPIO 22 goes HIGH), trigger a separate function to log the event.

    *   A press of the button should toggle the state of *both* door locks.
    *   Motion detected by the PIR sensor should *only* unlock both door locks.

Each lock event (button press, motion detected, lock activation, lock deactivation) should be logged with a timestamp, lock ID (17 or 27), and action (e.g., "Button Press", "Motion Detected", "Lock 17 Activated", "Lock 27 Deactivated"). Use standard C library functions like `fopen()`, `fprintf()`, `time()`, and `localtime()` for file I/O and timestamp generation.

Include checks for file operations (e.g., `fopen()`). If `gpioInitialise()` fails, the program should exit gracefully with an error message.

8.  **Signal Handling:** Implement signal handling for `SIGINT` (Ctrl+C) to ensure that `gpioTerminate()` is called before the program exits, releasing the GPIO resources. Use `signal()` for signal handling.

9.  **Compilation:** The program should be compilable using `gcc` with the `-lpigpio` flag (e.g., `gcc -o door_control door_control.c -lpigpio -pthread`).

To create a C program for the Raspberry Pi Zero W that uses the `pigpio` library to control two 12V electromagnetic door locks, we need to follow these steps:

1. Initialize the GPIO pins.
2. Set up the push button for debouncing.
3. Control the door locks based on the button press.
4. Log the events to a file.

First, make sure you have the `pigpio` library installed. You can install it via `apt-get`:

```sh
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install pigpiod
```

Then, you can write the C program as follows:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <pigpio.h>
#include <time.h>

#define BUTTON_PIN 4
#define LOCK1_PIN 17
#define LOCK2_PIN 27

#define DEBOUNCE_TIME 200 // 200 microseconds

void log_event(int lock_id, char *action) {
    FILE *fp;
    time_t t;
    struct tm *tm_info;
    char buffer[80];

    time(&t);
    tm_info = localtime(&t);

    strftime(buffer, 80, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", tm_info);

    fp = fopen("lock_events.log", "a");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        perror("Failed to open lock_events.log");
        exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }

    fprintf(fp, "%s, Lock %d, %s\n", buffer, lock_id, action);
    fclose(fp);
}

int main(void) {
    int button_state, lock1_state, lock2_state;
    uint32_t start_time;

    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialization failed.\n");
        return -1;
    }

    gpioSetMode(BUTTON_PIN, PI_INPUT);
    gpioSetPullUpDown(BUTTON_PIN, PI_PUD_UP);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK1_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK2_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);

    while (1) {
        button_state = gpioRead(BUTTON_PIN);
        if (button_state == 0) { // Button is pressed
            start_time = micros();
            do {
                button_state = gpioRead(BUTTON_PIN);
                sleep(1); // Debounce delay
            } while (button_state == 0 && micros() - start_time < DEBOUNCE_TIME);

            if (button_state == 0) { // Button is still pressed after debounce
                // Toggle the state of the locks
                lock1_state = !gpioRead(LOCK1_PIN);
                lock2_state = !gpioRead(LOCK2_PIN);

                gpioWrite(LOCK1_PIN, lock1_state);
                gpioWrite(LOCK2_PIN, lock2_state);

                log_event(lock1_state ? 1 : 2, lock1_state ? "Unlock" : "Lock");
                log_event(lock2_state ? 1 : 2, lock2_state ? "Unlock" : "Lock");
            }
        }
    }

    gpioTerminate();

    return 0;
}
```

### Explanation:
1. **Initialization**: The program initializes the GPIO pins for the button and the locks. It sets up pull-up resistors on the button pin to ensure a clean reading when the button is not pressed.
2. **Debouncing**: The program checks the button state multiple times with a short delay to debounce the input. If the button is held down for more than the debounce time, it toggles the state of the locks.
3. **Logging**: The program logs the event to `lock_events.log`, including the timestamp, lock ID, and action taken (lock or unlock).
4. **Main Loop**: The program runs in an infinite loop, continuously monitoring the button state.

### Building and Running:
To compile the program, use the following command:

```sh
gcc -o door_lock_control door_lock_control.c -lpigpio
```

Then, run the compiled binary:

```sh
./door_lock_control
```

This program will keep running until you manually stop it. Make sure your locks are properly connected to the specified GPIO pins and that the `pigpiod` daemon is running.<|im_end|>

    if (gpioSetMode(BUTTON_GPIO, PI_INPUT) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to set GPIO %d as input\n", BUTTON_GPIO);
        cleanup_gpio();
        return 1;
    }

    if (gpioSetPullUpDown(BUTTON_GPIO, PI_PUD_UP) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to set pull-up resistor on GPIO %d\n", BUTTON_GPIO);
        cleanup_gpio();
        return 1;
    }

    if (gpioSetISRFuncEx(BUTTON_GPIO, FALLING_EDGE, 0, button_interrupt_handler, NULL) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to set ISR function on GPIO %d\n", BUTTON_GPIO);
        cleanup_gpio();
        return 1;
    }

    // Initialize locks
    locks[0].gpio = LOCK1_GPIO;
    locks[0].activated = 0;
    locks[0].last_activation = 0;

    locks[1].gpio = LOCK2_GPIO;
    locks[1].activated = 0;
    locks[1].last_activation = 0;

    // Open log file
    log_file = fopen(LOG_FILE, "a");
    if (log_file == NULL)
    {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to open log file %s\n", LOG_FILE);
        cleanup_gpio();
        return 1;
    }

    // Main loop
    while (running)
    {
        sleep(1); // Sleep for 1 second
    }

    // Cleanup before exiting
    cleanup_gpio();
    if (log_file != NULL)
    {
        fclose(log_file);
    }

    return 0;
}

// Button interrupt handler function
void button_interrupt_handler(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick)
{
    if (level == 0) // Button pressed (LOW)
    {
        time_t now = time(NULL);
        time_t diff = difftime(now, locks[gpio - 1].last_activation);

        if (diff > (DEBOUNCE_TIME / 1000.0))
        {
            int lock_id = locks[gpio - 1].gpio;
            if (!locks[gpio - 1].activated)
            {
                activate_lock(lock_id);
                locks[gpio - 1].activated = 1;
                locks[gpio - 1].last_activation = now;
            } else {
                deactivate_lock(lock_id);
                locks[gpio - 1].activated = 0;
                locks[gpio - 1].last_activation = now;
            }
        }
    }
}

// Function to activate a lock
void activate_lock(int lock_id)
{
    printf("Activating lock on GPIO %d\n", lock_id);
    gpioWrite(lock_id, 1); // Assuming HIGH activates the lock
    log_event(get_timestamp(), "Activated");
}

// Function to deactivate a lock
void deactivate_lock(int lock_id)
{
    printf("Deactivating lock on GPIO %d\n", lock_id);
    gpioWrite(lock_id, 0); // Assuming LOW deactivates the lock
    log_event(get_timestamp(), "Deactivated");
}

// Function to log an event to the log file
void log_event(const char *lock_id, const char *action)
{
    char *timestamp = get_timestamp();
    if (timestamp != NULL)
    {
        fprintf(log_file, "%s,%s,%s\n", timestamp, lock_id, action);
        fflush(log_file); // Ensure data is written immediately
        free(timestamp);
    } else {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to get timestamp, cannot log event\n");
    }
}

// Function to get the current timestamp
char* get_timestamp()
{
    time_t rawtime;
    struct tm *timeinfo;
    char *timestamp = (char*)malloc(20 * sizeof(char)); // Allocate memory for the timestamp string

    if (timestamp == NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to allocate memory for timestamp\n");
        return NULL;
    }

    time(&rawtime);
    timeinfo = localtime(&rawtime);

    strftime(timestamp, 20, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", timeinfo);
    return timestamp;
}

// Function to clean up GPIO resources
void cleanup_gpio()
{
    // Deactivate any active locks
    if (locks[0].activated) {
        deactivate_lock(LOCK1_GPIO);
    }
    if (locks[1].activated) {
        deactivate_lock(LOCK2_GPIO);
    }

    // Release GPIO pins
    gpioSetMode(LOCK1_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK2_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT);

    // Terminate pigpio library
    gpioTerminate();
}<|im_end|>

```c
#include <stdio.h>

#include <pigpio.h>

                sleep(0.01); // Wait 10ms
            }

            printf("Button pressed\n");

            // Toggle the locks
            int state = digitalRead(LOCK_1_GPIO);
            digitalWrite(LOCK_1_GPIO, !state);
            state = digitalRead(LOCK_2_GPIO);
            digitalWrite(LOCK_2_GPIO, !state);

        }
    }

    closeGPIO();
    return 0;
}

        return 0;

    gpioSetPullUpDown(BUTTON_GPIO, PI_PUD_UP);

    // Set locks as outputs
    gpioSetMode(LOCK_1_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK_2_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT);

    // Return success
    return 1;
}

void closeGPIO() {
    // Cleanup GPIO
    gpioTerminate();
}

void logEvent(const char* format, ...) {
    // Create a buffer to hold the log message
    char buffer[256];
    time_t now;

    // Get current timestamp
    now = time(NULL);

    // Format the timestamp and additional information
    strftime(buffer, sizeof(buffer), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S ", localtime(&now));
    va_list args;
    va_start(args, format);
    vsnprintf(buffer + strlen(buffer), sizeof(buffer) - strlen(buffer), format, args);
    va_end(args);

    // Append the log message to the file
    FILE *logFile = fopen("lock_events.log", "a");
    if (logFile) {
        fprintf(logFile, "%s\n", buffer);
        fclose(logFile);
    }
}
```

#define LOCK2_PIN 27

// Define log file path
#define LOG_FILE "lock_events.log"

// Global variable to store the last button press time
static volatile unsigned int last_button_press_time = 0;

// Function prototypes
void initialize_gpio();
void cleanup_gpio();
void button_interrupt_handler(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick);
void activate_lock(int lock_id);
void deactivate_lock(int lock_id);
void log_event(int lock_id, const char *action);

// Structure to hold configuration data
typedef struct {
    int button_pin;
    int lock1_pin;
    int lock2_pin;
    int debounce_time;
    char *log_file;
} Config;

// Global configuration
Config config = {BUTTON_PIN, LOCK1_PIN, LOCK2_PIN, DEBOUNCE_TIME, LOG_FILE};

int main() {
    // Initialize pigpio
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Initialize GPIO pins
    initialize_gpio();

    // Set up interrupt handler for button press
    if (gpioSetAlertFuncEx(config.button_pin, button_interrupt_handler, NULL) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to set alert function for button pin\n");
        cleanup_gpio();
        return 1;
    }

    printf("Door lock control system started. Press Ctrl+C to exit.\n");

    // Main loop
    while (1) {
        // Keep the program running
        gpioDelay(100); // Sleep for 100ms to reduce CPU usage
    }

    return 0;
}

// Initialize GPIO pins
void initialize_gpio() {
    // Set button pin as input with pull-up resistor
    gpioSetMode(config.button_pin, PI_INPUT);
    gpioSetPullUpDown(config.button_pin, PI_PUD_UP);

    // Set lock pins as output
    gpioSetMode(config.lock1_pin, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(config.lock2_pin, PI_OUTPUT);

    // Initialize locks to OFF state
    gpioWrite(config.lock1_pin, 0);
    gpioWrite(config.lock2_pin, 0);

    printf("GPIO pins initialized.\n");
}

// Button interrupt handler
void button_interrupt_handler(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick) {
    static uint32_t last_tick = 0;

    // Get current time
    time_t now = time(NULL);
    struct tm *tstruct = localtime(&now);
    char buf[80];
    strftime(buf, sizeof(buf), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", tstruct);

    // Debouncing logic
    if (tick - last_button_press_time > config.debounce_time * 1000) {
        printf("%s: Button pressed on GPIO %d\n", buf, gpio);

        // Activate or deactivate locks based on the button state
        if (level == 0) { // Button is pressed (LOW)
            last_button_press_time = tick;
            activate_lock(1);
            log_event(1, "Activate");
            gpioDelay(2000); // Wait 2 seconds
            deactivate_lock(1);
            log_event(1, "Deactivate");
        } else {
            printf("Button not pressed, ignoring\n");
        }
    }
    last_tick = tick;
}

// Activate a lock
void activate_lock(int lock_id) {
    if (lock_id == 1) {
        gpioWrite(config.lock1_pin, 1);
        printf("Lock 1 activated\n");
    } else if (lock_id == 2) {
        gpioWrite(config.lock2_pin, 1);
        printf("Lock 2 activated\n");
    } else {
        fprintf(stderr, "Invalid lock ID: %d\n", lock_id);
    }
}

// Deactivate a lock
void deactivate_lock(int lock_id) {
    if (lock_id == 1) {
        gpioWrite(config.lock1_pin, 0);
        printf("Lock 1 deactivated\n");
    } else if (lock_id == 2) {
        gpioWrite(config.lock2_pin, 0);
        printf("Lock 2 deactivated\n");
    } else {
        fprintf(stderr, "Invalid lock ID: %d\n", lock_id);
    }
}

// Log an event to the log file
void log_event(int lock_id, const char *action) {
    FILE *fp = fopen(config.log_file, "a");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error opening log file!\n");
        return;
    }

    time_t now = time(NULL);
    struct tm *tstruct = localtime(&now);
    char buf[80];
    strftime(buf, sizeof(buf), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", tstruct);

    fprintf(fp, "%s Lock %d %s\n", buf, lock_id, action);
    fclose(fp);
}

// Cleanup GPIO resources
void cleanup_gpio() {
    // Disable interrupts
    gpioSetAlertFuncEx(config.button_pin, NULL, NULL);

    // Deactivate locks
    gpioWrite(config.lock1_pin, 0);
    gpioWrite(config.lock2_pin, 0);

    // Terminate pigpio
    gpioTerminate();

    printf("GPIO resources released. Exiting.\n");
}

// Signal handler for Ctrl+C
void signal_handler(int signal) {
    printf("Caught signal %d\n", signal);
    cleanup_gpio();
    exit(0);
}

// Function to read the current state of the button pin
int read_button_state() {
    return gpioRead(config.button_pin);
}<|im_end|>

1. Initialize the GPIO pins.
2. Set up the push button with debouncing.
3. Control the door locks based on button presses.

First, ensure you have the `pigpio` library installed on your Raspberry Pi. You can install it using:


Here is the C program:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

#define BUTTON_PIN 4
#define LOCK1_PIN 17
#define LOCK2_PIN 27

#define LOG_FILE "lock_events.log"

int init_gpio() {
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        perror("Failed to initialize GPIO");
        return -1;
    }
    gpioSetMode(BUTTON_PIN, PI_INPUT);
    gpioSetPullUpDown(BUTTON_PIN, PI_PUD_UP);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK1_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK2_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);

    return 0;
}

void log_event(int lock_id, const char *action) {
    FILE *fp = fopen(LOG_FILE, "a");
    if (!fp) {
        perror("Failed to open log file");
        return;
    }

    time_t now = time(NULL);
    struct tm *tm_info = localtime(&now);
    fprintf(fp, "%04d-%02d-%02d %02d:%02d:%02d Lock %d %s\n",
            tm_info->tm_year + 1900, tm_info->tm_mon + 1, tm_info->tm_mday,
            tm_info->tm_hour, tm_info->tm_min, tm_info->tm_sec,
            lock_id, action);

    fclose(fp);
}

int main() {
    if (init_gpio() < 0) {
        return -1;
    }

    int button_state = 1; // Initial state: button not pressed
    int lock1_state = 0;
    int lock2_state = 0;

    while (1) {
        int new_button_state = gpioRead(BUTTON_PIN);

        if (new_button_state == 0 && button_state == 1) {
            // Button was just pressed
            if (lock1_state == 0) {
                // Lock1 is currently unlocked, lock it
                gpioWrite(LOCK1_PIN, 1);
                log_event(1, "Locked");
                lock1_state = 1;
            } else {
                // Lock1 is currently locked, unlock it
                gpioWrite(LOCK1_PIN, 0);
                log_event(1, "Unlocked");
                lock1_state = 0;
            }

            if (lock2_state == 0) {
                // Lock2 is currently unlocked, lock it
                gpioWrite(LOCK2_PIN, 1);
                log_event(2, "Locked");
                lock2_state = 1;
            } else {
                // Lock2 is currently locked, unlock it
                gpioWrite(LOCK2_PIN, 0);
                log_event(2, "Unlocked");
                lock2_state = 0;
            }
        }

        button_state = new_button_state;
        gpioDelay(100); // Small delay to reduce CPU usage
```

#include <stdio.h>

#include <fcntl.h>
#include <sys/ioctl.h>
#include <linux/i2c-dev.h>

#include <pigpio.h>

// Configuration structure
typedef struct {
    int lock1_gpio;
    int lock2_gpio;
    int button_gpio;
    char *log_file;
} Config;

// Global variables (minimize usage)
static volatile int running = 1;
static FILE *log_fp = NULL;

// Function prototypes
void signal_handler(int signal);
int load_config(const char *config_file, Config *config);
char* get_timestamp();
int write_to_log(const char *message);
int initialize_gpio(Config *config);
int read_button_state(int gpio);
int activate_lock(int gpio, int duration_ms);
void cleanup_gpio();

// Signal handler for graceful shutdown
void signal_handler(int signal) {
    if (signal == SIGINT) {
        printf("Caught SIGINT, exiting gracefully...\n");
        running = 0;
    }
}

// Load configuration from file
int load_config(const char *config_file, Config *config) {
    FILE *fp = fopen(config_file, "r");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        perror("Error opening config file");
        return -1;
    }

    char line[256];
    while (fgets(line, sizeof(line), fp)) {
        // Remove newline character
        line[strcspn(line, "\n")] = 0;

        // Skip comments and empty lines
        if (line[0] == '#' || line[0] == '\0') continue;

        char *key = strtok(line, "=");
        char *value = strtok(NULL, "=");

        if (key != NULL && value != NULL) {
            if (strcmp(key, "lock1_gpio") == 0) {
                config->lock1_gpio = atoi(value);
            } else if (strcmp(key, "lock2_gpio") == 0) {
                config->lock2_gpio = atoi(value);
            } else if (strcmp(key, "button_gpio") == 0) {
                config->button_gpio = atoi(value);
            } else if (strcmp(key, "log_file") == 0) {
                strncpy(config->log_file, value, sizeof(config->log_file) - 1);
                config->log_file[sizeof(config->log_file) - 1] = 0; // Ensure null termination
            }
        }
    }

    fclose(fp);
    return 0;
}

// Get current timestamp as a string
char* get_timestamp() {
    time_t now = time(NULL);
    struct tm tstruct;
    localtime_r(&now, &tstruct);
    char *buf = malloc(20 * sizeof(char)); // Allocate memory for the timestamp
    if (buf == NULL) {
        perror("Failed to allocate memory for timestamp");
        return NULL;
    }
    strftime(buf, 20, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", &tstruct);
    return buf;
}

// Write message to log file
int write_to_log(const char *message) {
    char *timestamp = get_timestamp();
    if (timestamp == NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to get timestamp\n");
        return -1;
    }

    fprintf(log_fp, "[%s] %s\n", timestamp, message);
    fflush(log_fp); // Ensure data is written immediately

    free(timestamp);
    return 0;
}

// Initialize GPIO pins
int initialize_gpio(Config *config) {
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return -1;
    }

    gpioSetMode(config->lock1_gpio, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(config->lock2_gpio, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(config->button_gpio, PI_INPUT);
    gpioSetPullUpDown(config->button_gpio, PI_PUD_UP); // Enable pull-up resistor

    return 0;
}

// Read button state with debouncing
int read_button_state(int gpio) {
    static int last_state = 1; // Assuming initial state is HIGH
    static unsigned long last_debounce_time = 0;
    unsigned long debounce_delay = 20; // Debounce delay in milliseconds
    int current_state = gpioRead(gpio);

    if (current_state != last_state) {
        last_debounce_time = gpioTick();
    }

    if ((gpioTick() - last_debounce_time) > debounce_delay * 1000) {
        last_state = current_state;
        return current_state;
    }

    return last_state;
}

// Activate lock for specified duration
int activate_lock(int gpio, int duration_ms) {
    if (gpioWrite(gpio, 1) != 0) { // Activate lock (HIGH)
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to activate lock on GPIO %d\n", gpio);
        return -1;
    }

    gpioDelay(duration_ms * 1000); // Delay in microseconds

    if (gpioWrite(gpio, 0) != 0) { // Deactivate lock (LOW)
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to deactivate lock on GPIO %d\n", gpio);
        return -1;
    }

    return 0;
}

// Cleanup GPIO pins before exiting
void cleanup_gpio() {
    gpioTerminate();
    if (log_fp != NULL) {
        fclose(log_fp);
    }
}

int main() {
    Config config;

    // Load configuration
    if (load_config("config.txt", &config) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to load configuration\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Open log file
    log_fp = fopen(config.log_file, "a");
    if (log_fp == NULL) {
        perror("Error opening log file");
        return 1;
    }

    // Set up signal handler
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);

    // Initialize GPIO
    if (initialize_gpio(&config) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "GPIO initialization failed\n");
        cleanup_gpio();
        return 1;
    }

    printf("Door lock controller started. Press Ctrl+C to exit.\n");

    while (running) {
        if (read_button_state(config.button_gpio) == 0) { // Button pressed (LOW)
            char *timestamp = get_timestamp();
            if (timestamp == NULL) {
                fprintf(stderr, "Failed to get timestamp\n");
                continue; // Skip to next iteration
            }

            int lock_id = 0;
            if (gpioRead(config.lock1_gpio) == 0) {
                lock_id = 1;
            } else if (gpioRead(config.lock2_gpio) == 0) {
                lock_id = 2;
            }

            char *action = "unknown";
            if (lock_id == 1) {
                action = "released";
            } else if (lock_id == 2) {
                action = "released";
            }

            if (action != "unknown") {
                char *message = malloc(strlen(timestamp) + strlen(config.log_file) + 50);
                if (message == NULL) {
                    perror("Failed to allocate memory for message");
                    free(timestamp);
                    continue; // Skip to next iteration
                }
                snprintf(message, strlen(timestamp) + strlen(config.log_file) + 50, "%s Lock %d %s", timestamp, lock_id, action);
                if (write_to_log(message) != 0) {
                    fprintf(stderr, "Failed to write to log\n");
                }
                free(message);
            }

            if (activate_lock(lock_id, 2000) != 0) {
                fprintf(stderr, "Failed to activate lock\n");
            }

            free(timestamp);
            gpioDelay(1000000); // Debounce delay (1 second)
        }
        gpioDelay(10000); // Check button state every 10ms
    }

    // Cleanup GPIO and close log file
    cleanup_gpio();

    printf("Door lock controller stopped.\n");

    return 0;
}<|im_end|>

prompt:
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi Zero W using the `pigpio` library to control two 12V electromagnetic door locks (connected to GPIO 17 and 27), triggered by a single momentary push button connected to GPIO 4 and a Passive Infrared (PIR) motion sensor connected to GPIO 22. Implement debouncing for the push button using `pigpio`'s callback features. The program should adopt a functional programming style, suitable for a beginner-level understanding, and designed for an interactive art installation.

Specifically:

1.  **GPIO Control:** Use `pigpio` functions (e.g., `gpioInitialise()`, `gpioSetMode()`, `gpioWrite()`, `gpioSetAlertFuncEx()`) to manage the GPIO pins. Initialize GPIO 17 and 27 as outputs for the door locks, GPIO 4 as an input with a pull-up resistor for the push button, and GPIO 22 as an input for the PIR sensor.

2.  **Button Debouncing:** Implement debouncing for the push button using `gpioSetAlertFuncEx()` on GPIO 4. This function should call a separate function when the button state changes, which handles the logic for triggering the door locks.

3.  **Motion Sensor Integration:** Monitor GPIO 22 (PIR sensor) using `gpioSetAlertFuncEx()`. When motion is detected (GPIO 22 goes HIGH), trigger a separate function to log the event.

4.  **Door Lock Control Logic:**
    *   A press of the button should toggle the state of *both* door locks.
    *   Motion detected by the PIR sensor should *only* unlock both door locks.
    *   Each door lock should be activated for a duration of 3 seconds. After 3 seconds, the lock should automatically deactivate. Use `pigpio`'s `gpioDelay()` function for timing.

5.  **Logging:** Create a log file named `lock_events.log`. Each lock event (button press, motion detected, lock activation, lock deactivation) should be logged with a timestamp, lock ID (17 or 27), and action (e.g., "Button Press", "Motion Detected", "Lock 17 Activated", "Lock 27 Deactivated"). Use standard C library functions like `fopen()`, `fprintf()`, `time()`, and `localtime()` for file I/O and timestamp generation.

6.  **Error Handling:** Implement robust error handling. Check the return values of `gpioInitialise()` and `gpioSetMode()` and log any errors to `lock_events.log` and `stderr`. Include checks for file operations (e.g., `fopen()`). If `gpioInitialise()` fails, the program should exit gracefully with an error message.

7.  **Functional Programming Style:** Structure the code into small, reusable functions. For example:
    *   `initGPIO()`: Initializes all GPIO pins.
    *   `toggleLocks()`: Toggles the state of both door locks.
    *   `activateLock(int gpio, int duration_ms)`: Activates a specific door lock for a given duration.
    *   `logEvent(int gpio, const char *action)`: Logs an event to `lock_events.log`.
    *   `buttonCallback(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick)`: Handles the button press event.
    *   `motionCallback(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick)`: Handles the motion detection event.

8.  **Signal Handling:** Implement signal handling for `SIGINT` (Ctrl+C) to ensure that `gpioTerminate()` is called before the program exits, releasing the GPIO resources. Use `signal()` for signal handling.

9.  **Compilation:** The program should be compilable using `gcc` with the `-lpigpio` flag (e.g., `gcc -o door_control door_control.c -lpigpio -pthread`).

10. **Libraries:** Strictly use `pigpio`, `stdio.h`, `stdlib.h`, `time.h`, and `signal.h`. Do not use any C++ libraries or non-standard/proprietary libraries.

11. **Adherence to Standards:** Ensure all function calls and library usage are compatible with Raspberry Pi OS (Bookworm/Bullseye) and avoid deprecated features.

12. **Context:** Remember this is for an interactive art installation, so consider the user experience (e.g., clear logging, reliable operation).
explanation: The code initializes GPIO pins for door locks, a button, and a PIR sensor using the `pigpio` library. It implements debouncing for the button and uses callbacks for button presses and motion detection.  Lock events are logged to a file, and signal handling ensures proper cleanup upon program termination.

To create a C program for controlling two 12V electromagnetic door locks on a Raspberry Pi Zero W using the `pigpio` library, we'll need to initialize GPIOs, handle debouncing for the push button, and log events to a file. Below is a complete example:

1. **Install the `pigpio` library**:
   - You can install `pigpio` via `apt-get` or compile it from source.
   - For `apt-get`, run: `sudo apt-get install pigpio`
   - Ensure you have root access since `pigpio` requires it.

2. **Write the C program**:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

#define LOCK1_PIN 17
#define LOCK2_PIN 27
#define BUTTON_PIN 4
#define LOG_FILE "lock_events.log"

// Function prototypes
void init_gpio();
void cleanup_gpio();
int read_button_debounced(int pin);
void log_event(const char *action, int lock_id);

int main(void) {
    // Initialize GPIO
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialization failed!\n");
        exit(1);
    }

    init_gpio();

    printf("Waiting for button press...\n");

    while (1) {
        int button_state = read_button_debounced(BUTTON_PIN);
        if (button_state == 1) {
            // Button pressed
            log_event("pressed", 0); // Log button press

            int lock1_state = gpioRead(LOCK1_PIN);
            int lock2_state = gpioRead(LOCK2_PIN);

            if (lock1_state == 0) {
                // Lock 1 is open, close it
                gpioWrite(LOCK1_PIN, 1);
                log_event("closed", 1);
            } else {
                // Lock 1 is closed, open it
                gpioWrite(LOCK1_PIN, 0);
                log_event("opened", 1);
            }

            if (lock2_state == 0) {
                // Lock 2 is open, close it
                gpioWrite(LOCK2_PIN, 1);
                log_event("closed", 2);
            } else {
                // Lock 2 is closed, open it
                gpioWrite(LOCK2_PIN, 0);
                log_event("opened", 2);
            }
        }
        sleep(1); // Debounce delay
    }

    return 0;
}

void init_gpio() {
    // Set up GPIO pins as outputs
    gpioSetMode(LOCK1_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK2_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(BUTTON_PIN, PI_INPUT);

    // Set initial states of the locks
    gpioWrite(LOCK1_PIN, 1);
    gpioWrite(LOCK2_PIN, 1);
}

void cleanup_gpio() {
    // Clean up GPIO settings
    gpioSetMode(LOCK1_PIN, PI_INPUT);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK2_PIN, PI_INPUT);
    gpioSetMode(BUTTON_PIN, PI_INPUT);
    gpioTerminate();
}

int read_button_debounced(int pin) {
    int state = 0;
    static int last_state = 0;
    static long last_time = 0;

    long now = gpioTick();

    if ((now - last_time) > 1000000) { // Debounce time: 1 second
        state = gpioRead(pin);
        last_time = now;
    }

    if (state != last_state) {
        last_state = state;
        return state;
    } else {
        return 0; // No change detected
    }
}

void log_event(const char *action, int lock_id) {
    FILE *log_file = fopen(LOG_FILE, "a");
    if (!log_file) {
        perror("Failed to open log file");
        return;
    }

    struct tm *time_info;
    time_t raw_time;

    time(&raw_time);
    time_info = localtime(&raw_time);

    fprintf(log_file, "%s %04d-%02d-%02d %02d:%02d:%02d %d %s\n",
            action,
            time_info->tm_year + 1900,
            time_info->tm_mon + 1,
            time_info->tm_mday,
            time_info->tm_hour,
            time_info->tm_min,
            time_info->tm_sec,
            lock_id,
            action);

    fclose(log_file);
}
```

### Explanation:
- **GPIO Initialization**: The `init_gpio()` function sets the GPIO modes for the lock and button pins.
- **Debouncing**: The `read_button_debounced()` function ensures that the button press is only detected once per debounce period.
- **Logging**: The `log_event()` function logs the event to `lock_events.log` with a timestamp and lock ID.
- **Main Loop**: The `main()` function continuously checks for button presses and toggles the state of the locks accordingly.

### Notes:
- Ensure your Raspberry Pi is properly configured and has internet access if you're installing libraries via `apt-get`.
- Adjust the debounce time in `read_button_debounced()` according to your needs.
- Make sure to compile the program with the appropriate flags to link against `libpigpio`.

To compile the program, use:
```sh
gcc -o lock_controller lock_controller.c -lpigpio
```

Then run the compiled binary:
```sh
sudo ./lock_controller
```

This setup should allow you to control two electromagnetic door locks with a single button press, while logging all actions to a file.<|im_end|>

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <signal.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

// Define GPIO pins
#define BUTTON_GPIO 4
#define LOCK1_GPIO 17
#define LOCK2_GPIO 27

// Debounce time in milliseconds
#define DEBOUNCE_TIME 50

// Log file name
#define LOG_FILE "lock_events.log"

// Global variables
volatile int button_pressed = 0;
FILE *log_file = NULL;

// Function prototypes
void button_callback(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick, void *userdata);
int initialize_gpio();
int open_lock(int gpio);
int close_lock(int gpio);
void log_event(int lock_id, const char *action);
void signal_handler(int signal);

// Structure to hold button debounce information
typedef struct {
    unsigned long last_tick;
} ButtonState;

ButtonState button_state = {0};

int main() {
    // Initialize pigpio
    if (initialize_gpio() != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to initialize pigpio. Exiting.\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Set button pin as input with pull-up resistor
    gpioSetMode(BUTTON_GPIO, PI_INPUT);
    gpioSetPullUpDown(BUTTON_GPIO, PI_PUD_UP);

    // Set lock pins as output
    gpioSetMode(LOCK1_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK2_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT);

    // Open log file
    log_file = fopen(LOG_FILE, "a");
    if (log_file == NULL) {
        perror("Error opening log file");
        gpioTerminate();
        return 1;
    }

    // Register signal handler for Ctrl+C
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);

    // Set interrupt callback for button press
    gpioSetAlertFuncEx(BUTTON_GPIO, button_callback, NULL);

    printf("Door lock control system started. Press Ctrl+C to exit.\n");

    // Main loop
    while (1) {
        gpioDelay(100); // Sleep to reduce CPU usage
    }

    return 0;
}

// Interrupt callback function
void button_callback(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick, void *userdata) {
    static unsigned long last_tick = 0;
    unsigned long current_tick = gpioTick();

    // Debouncing
    if (current_tick - last_tick < DEBOUNCE_TIME * 1000) {
        return;
    }
    last_tick = current_tick;

    if (level == 0) { // Button pressed (LOW)
        button_pressed = 1;
        gpioDelay(100); // Small delay to ensure button is released before next read

        // Check which lock to open based on the order in which they were initialized
        int lock1_state = gpioRead(LOCK1_GPIO);
        int lock2_state = gpioRead(LOCK2_GPIO);

        if (lock1_state == 0 && lock2_state == 0) { // Both locks are closed
            if (open_lock(LOCK1_GPIO) == 0) {
                log_event(1, "OPEN");
            } else {
                log_event(1, "ERROR OPENING LOCK 1");
            }
        } else if (lock2_state == 0) { // Lock 1 is open, try to open lock 2
            if (open_lock(LOCK2_GPIO) == 0) {
                log_event(2, "OPEN");
            } else {
                log_event(2, "ERROR OPENING LOCK 2");
            }
        } else { // All locks are open
            if (close_lock(LOCK1_GPIO) == 0) {
                log_event(1, "CLOSE");
            } else {
                log_event(1, "ERROR CLOSING LOCK 1");
            }

            if (close_lock(LOCK2_GPIO) == 0) {
                log_event(2, "CLOSE");
            } else {
                log_event(2, "ERROR CLOSING LOCK 2");
            }
        }
    }
}

// Initialize GPIO pins
int initialize_gpio() {
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    return 0;
}

// Open door lock
int open_lock(int gpio) {
    if (gpioWrite(gpio, 1) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to write to GPIO %d\n", gpio);
        return 1;
    }
    gpioDelay(1000); // Keep lock open for 1 second
    if (gpioWrite(gpio, 0) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to write to GPIO %d\n", gpio);
        return 1;
    }
    return 0;
}

// Close door lock
int close_lock(int gpio) {
    if (gpioWrite(gpio, 1) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to write to GPIO %d\n", gpio);
        return 1;
    }
    return 0;
}

// Log event to file
void log_event(int lock_id, const char *action) {
    time_t now = time(NULL);
    struct tm tstruct;
    localtime_r(&now, &tstruct);
    char buf[80];
    strftime(buf, sizeof(buf), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", &tstruct);

    if (log_file != NULL) {
        fprintf(log_file, "%s - Lock %d - %s\n", buf, lock_id, action);
        fflush(log_file); // Ensure data is written to disk immediately
    } else {
        fprintf(stderr, "Log file is not open!\n");
    }
}

// Signal handler for Ctrl+C
void signal_handler(int signal) {
    printf("Ctrl+C detected. Exiting gracefully...\n");

    // Release GPIO resources
    gpioTerminate();

    // Close log file
    if (log_file != NULL) {
        fclose(log_file);
    }

    exit(0);
}<|im_end|>
